sections.title: Make Malas
sections.prose_summary: Children make necklaces (malas) using 8 beads of two colours — 4 of each — by physically stringing or colouring bead outlines. The section introduces symmetry through the bead necklace: a mala is symmetrical when its two halves are exactly the same. Students then identify and construct symmetrical malas with different bead combinations (6+2), placing this squarely on the concrete end of the concrete → pictorial → symbolic path.

tasks.description: Colour the beads in the strings using two colours to show the malas that you have made (4 beads of one colour, 4 of another).
On the previous page, tick the malas that are symmetrical.
Tick the malas that are symmetrical and cross the one(s) that are not symmetrical from the given bead-string pictures.
Use 6 beads of one colour and 2 beads of another colour to make symmetrical malas.

sections.title: Rangolis All Around
sections.prose_summary: The section uses kolam (a Tamil Nadu tradition) and rangoli patterns from different states — Pookalam (Kerala) and Aipan (Uttarakhand) — to deepen understanding of symmetry. Children observe, trace, fold, and draw rangolis to find lines of symmetry and complete the missing half of a pattern. The progression moves from concrete manipulation (tracing and folding paper) to pictorial work (drawing lines and completing patterns on a dot grid), solidifying the idea that a symmetrical figure can be divided into two identical halves.

tasks.description: Observe the rangolis given below. Are all rangolis symmetrical?
Trace these rangolis on a paper. Fold the tracing paper in such a way that one half of the rangoli lies exactly on the other half.
Draw lines in the given rangolis that divide them into two identical halves.
Look for other symmetrical things around you. Discuss.
Draw and complete the symmetrical rangolis given below (three rangoli patterns — (a), (b), (c) — with one half provided).
Draw some more rangolis in your notebook that are symmetrical.

sections.title: Make Masks
sections.prose_summary: Children make a symmetrical cat mask by folding a paper in half, drawing half a cat on one side, cutting it out, then opening the fold to reveal a symmetrical mask. The contrast between Soni's symmetrical mask and Avi's non-symmetrical one (where one eye-hole is misaligned so he can see only with one eye) makes the concept vivid and functional. This is a concrete making activity that embeds symmetry in a craft context.

tasks.description: Fold paper along the middle, draw half a cat, cut it out, open the fold, make eyes and nose, colour it and attach a rubber band — your symmetrical cat mask is ready.

sections.title: Tit for Tat
sections.prose_summary: A humorous story about a painter who draws only half a picture and asks for full payment, using a mirror to show the 'complete' image. Soni turns the trick on the painter with half the money. Students must identify which objects would defeat the painter's trick — i.e., objects that are not symmetrical, so the mirror image would be incorrect. This pushes children to think about non-symmetry and what makes an object's halves different.

tasks.description: What is the trick the painter is playing? Find things for the painter to draw so that he can no longer play the trick. Draw three such things here.

sections.title: The Mirror Game
sections.prose_summary: Children play a two-player counter game on a 4×4 grid divided by a mirror line. One player places counters on their side; the other must place counters on their side to form the mirror image. A physical mirror is used to verify. The game builds the concept of mirror reflection dynamically — children move from placing counters (concrete exploration) to checking whether an arrangement is a correct mirror image (spatial reasoning).

tasks.description: Play the mirror game: one player places four counters on their side of the grid; the other places counters on their side as the mirror image. Switch roles and repeat.
Has Avi placed the counters at the right places? Check it by placing the mirror on the line drawn.

sections.title: Symmetry Puzzles and Tiling Exploration
sections.prose_summary: Four connected reasoning tasks push children to apply symmetry thinking in novel ways: identifying the odd-one-out from pairs of shape-and-dot arrangements, filling a grid to create a mirror-image pattern using a limited set of coloured cells, rearranging balls to make one side mirror the other (with only three moves allowed), and deciding which shape cutouts tessellate to fill a hexagon. The tasks collectively require children to compare, deduce, and apply spatial logic beyond routine identification.

tasks.description: Pick the odd one out and give reasons (from four pairs of shape-and-dot arrangements).
Fill 4 boxes with red colour and 3 with blue colour in such a way that one side is the mirror image of the other. In how many ways can you fill it?
Make Micy's side the same as that of Catty's side. You can rearrange only three balls in Micy's side.
Which shape cutouts would fit in the given hexagon shape without overlapping and gaps?

sections.title: Tiling the Paths
sections.prose_summary: Sparked by grandpa's foot getting stuck in a gap between tiles, children use rangometry shapes (manipulatives) to fill given outlines — a rectangle, a square, and a hexagon — with no gaps or overlaps, discovering which shapes tessellate. They then create their own composite tiles by joining two or more rangometry shapes, trace them repeatedly to make paths, and attempt to replicate three given tessellation patterns. The section sits at the concrete-to-pictorial boundary, anchored in physical shape manipulation.

tasks.description: Use rangometry shapes to fill the given shapes (rectangle, square, hexagon) with no gaps and overlaps.
Use two or more rangometry shapes to create your tiles. Now, trace the tiles to create different paths.
Try making these paths (three given tessellation patterns using repeated tiles).

sections.title: Giant Wheel — Reading the Fair Map
sections.prose_summary: Soni and Avi use a pictorial map of the Surajkund fair to navigate from the entry to the chaupal where their grandparents are waiting. The map has a legend with symbols for parking, ATM, restaurant, shops, giant wheel, pond, play area, and chaupal. Students read the map to decode symbols, identify places, count exit routes, follow a multi-step colour-coded path, and give directions from one point to another. The section introduces basic spatial navigation and directional language (left, right, forward) through a real-world map context.

tasks.description: Imagine sitting with Soni and Avi. Think of a place or stall, and challenge your friend to find out which stall you have in mind. Help them guess by answering yes or no.
Which place does the chaupal sign show?
Circle the picture in the map that shows the play area.
Which place does the parking sign show?
How many exit routes are there in the fair?
Follow the path Avi and Soni are following: walk on blue lane, turn right on green lane, take left towards red lane, take first left on golden lane, pass stalls to find the chaupal.
An uncle asks Dada ji the way to the ATM. Tell him the way to the ATM from the chaupal.

sections.title: Exit Maze
sections.prose_summary: As the family leaves the fair, children encounter a maze with two possible exits — one a winding maze path, one direct. Students navigate the maze from the fair entrance to the exit, tracking objects encountered along the way (a snake, a dog, a pineapple, a kite, a star). Writing about the path taken introduces descriptive directional language. The section wraps up spatial reasoning in a playful problem-solving context.

tasks.description: Follow the maze with Soni and Avi to exit the fair.
Share the way you went through the maze. Write the things you found on the way.
